title: The Lost Opus
blurb: A stolen symphony. A stolen name. Vienna’s gilded elite will burn to protect their lies. Elias Voss plays the ghost—until the score calls him back to claim his throne.
comparable: For fans of *The Song of Achilles* by Madeline Miller, *The Picture of Dorian Gray* by Oscar Wilde

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

Café Central smelled of yesterday’s cigars and today’s ink. Elias Voss sat in his usual corner, fingers hovering over the keys of an old Blüthner, playing not for the room but for the man across from him—Herr Bauer, a banker who liked to believe he understood music.

“Your phrasing lacks conviction,” Bauer said, swirling coffee that had long gone cold. “A minor key should not sound like a question.”

Elias’s smile didn’t reach his eyes. “Then it’s fortunate I didn’t ask your opinion.”

The banker’s jaw tightened. Across the café, the Vienna Philharmonic’s maestro held court at a long mahogany table, surrounded by sycophants. Gregor Licht’s laugh boomed like a gong, full of warmth he reserved only for those who mattered. Elias had once thought that warmth genuine. Now he knew better.

A violin case rested against his chair. He tapped his foot against its scuffed leather. The case held no violin. Inside was the original score of *Requiem for a Lost Dawn*, wrapped in oilcloth, its pages yellowed with age, the Voss family crest stamped in faded gilt on the cover.

Bauer leaned in. “You’re not concentrating.”

“I am exactly where I need to be.”

Outside, a tram rattled past, rattling the café’s windows. Elias’s melody stumbled—just once—before resetting. Bauer’s fingers drummed the table. The man had paid for this lesson. He expected brilliance.

Then Licht stood, adjusting his cuffs, and the room’s temperature rose with him. He moved through the café like royalty, stopping to clap a patron on the shoulder, accepting congratulations on some unseen triumph. His gaze swept the room, dismissing the mediocre, lingering on the promising. It paused on Elias.

For a breath, their eyes met. Licht’s smile didn’t waver, but his grip on his cane tightened—a fraction, nothing more.

Elias looked down. Played the next phrase perfectly.

Isabella Dvořáková slipped into the seat beside Bauer, her dark coat shedding flakes of snow. She carried a notebook, its pages already filled with scribbled observations. She nodded at Elias, not in greeting, but in acknowledgment. The critic’s presence here was no accident. She had come to judge.

“Herr Voss,” she said, voice smooth as fresh sheet music. “Your playing is… precise. But precision is not art.”

Elias didn’t stop playing. “Then art must be your weakness, Fräulein Dvořáková. You mistake vagueness for depth.”

She laughed, low and knowing. “Perhaps. But depth requires risk. Do you take risks?”

“Only when necessary.”

Bauer cleared his throat. “I should be going. My wife expects me.”

He left a stack of schillings on the table, more than the lesson was worth. Elias didn’t stop him.

Isabella opened her notebook. “They say *Requiem for a Lost Dawn* is Licht’s greatest work.”

“They say a lot of things.”

“They say it’s a modern masterpiece. Unshackled from the past.”

Elias’s fingers missed a note. The café’s murmur swallowed the error.

“You’re trembling,” Isabella observed.

He curled his left hand into a fist beneath the table. The scar tissue along his knuckles—a souvenir from the night he learned Licht had stolen more than credit. “Cold.”

“It’s May.”

“Vienna.”

She smiled, tapped her pencil against the notebook. “You play like a ghost. Not the kind that haunts concert halls, but the kind that lingers in attics. Half-alive.”

Elias turned to the window. Reflected in the glass, Licht’s party was breaking up. Clara Weiss lingered, her bow in hand, her expression unreadable. She met Elias’s gaze in the windowpane. Held it for a heartbeat too long.

Then Licht placed a hand on her shoulder, guiding her away. She didn’t shrug it off.

Isabella watched the exchange. “Clara’s loyal. Even when it costs her.”

“And what does loyalty cost you, Fräulein?”

“Everything, eventually.”

Elias exhaled. Played the final chord, held it until the last tremor faded.

Bauer’s schillings remained on the table. Isabella snapped her notebook shut. “You should leave,” she said. “Before you embarrass yourself.”

He stood. The violin case stayed on the chair.

Outside, the snow had stopped. Vienna’s streets gleamed under gaslight. Elias walked briskly, his gloved hands clenched. The case banged against his leg with every step.

He turned onto Dorotheergasse. The music shop’s sign creaked in the wind. Above it, his attic window was dark.

Inside the shop, Herr Meissner looked up from tuning a violin. “You’re late, Herr Voss.”

“I know.”

He took the stairs two at a time, the case growing heavier with each step. At his door, he paused. Listened.

Nothing.

Unlocked the door. Stepped inside.

The room was cold. The fire had died. On his desk, half-covered by sheet music, lay a single sheet of paper.

It wasn’t his.

The handwriting was Licht’s—neat, measured, arrogant. Elias didn’t pick it up. Didn’t need to.

But the melody on the page was unmistakable.

The opening bars of *Requiem for a Lost Dawn*.

Licht had brought it here.

Elias’s breath came shallow. He set his case on the table. Unwrapped the score. Held the real *Requiem* in his hands.

Then he heard footsteps on the stairs.

Fast. Purposeful.

He tucked the Voss score beneath a stack of blank manuscript paper. Turned just as the door flew open.

Clara stood there, her face pale. “They’re performing it,” she said. “Next month. Licht’s autumn festival. He’s calling it his magnum opus.”

Elias didn’t move. “He always does.”

She stepped inside. Closed the door behind her. “He found your copy. In the Philharmonic archives. He knows.”

For the first time in years, Elias Voss felt the old fire. Not the slow burn of composition, but the white-hot flame of vengeance.

Clara’s hands shook. “You have to stop him.”

Elias looked at the manuscript in his hands. Touched the crest.

Then he folded the paper carefully, slipping it into his coat pocket.

“No,” he said. “I have to finish it.”

Chapter 2

The Golden Hall of the Musikverein smelled of polished wood and the faint metallic tang of violins fresh from their cases. The Vienna Philharmonic filled the stage in waves—warm-up scales rolling like distant thunder, the scent of rosin and old sheet music thick in the air. Elias Voss stood in the doorway, baton dangling from his fingers, watching Maestro Gregor Licht’s back as the man circled the podium like a general inspecting his troops.

Clara Weiss stood near the front, violin tucked beneath her chin, eyes fixed on the score. She didn’t look up.

Isabella Dvořáková sat in the first row of empty seats, notebook open, pen poised. Her gaze flicked to Elias—just once—before she scribbled something with a satisfied nod.

Licht clapped his hands once. The room fell silent.

“Today,” he announced, voice carrying without effort, “we begin rehearsal of my *Symphony No. 4 in D Minor*—a work that will define our season. But first, I want to address a matter of integrity.”

Elias’s fingers tightened around the baton. The wood bit into his palm.

Licht held up a score. The cover bore the Philharmonic’s emblem.

Not his. Not Licht’s.

*Requiem for a Lost Dawn.*

A murmur rippled through the musicians. Someone coughed. Clara’s bow rested against her knee, still.

“Some among us,” Licht said, “have been circulating rumors—foolish, dangerous whispers—that this piece is not mine.” He paused. Smiled. “I won’t dignify such slander with a direct response. But I will remind you all that art is not a matter of bloodlines or birthright. It is craft. Discipline. And, above all, truth.”

He tapped the score against his palm. The leather cover made a soft *thwack* each time.

“Therefore, we will rehearse it today. Not as a tribute, but as a lesson.”

Elias stepped forward. The floorboards creaked beneath his boots.

Licht turned. His smile didn’t falter. “Ah. Herr Voss. I should have known you’d arrive just in time to witness greatness.”

The musicians shifted. Some looked away. Others stole glances.

Elias didn’t speak.

Licht gestured to the empty podium. “Feel free to conduct. Though I warn you—I’ve already set the tempo. And the tempo is *allegro agitato*. Not your usual languid waltz.”

A few stifled laughs. Clara’s jaw clenched.

Elias set his baton on the podium. Untied his coat. Hung it on a chair.

Then he turned to the orchestra.

“Play it,” he said. “The *Requiem*. From the beginning.”

Isabella’s pen froze mid-stroke.

Licht’s eyebrows climbed. “I beg your pardon?”

“You want to teach a lesson?” Elias kept his voice level. “Then show them how it’s done. Play *Requiem for a Lost Dawn*.”

The musicians exchanged glances. The concertmaster lowered her violin slightly.

Licht’s fingers twitched. “That piece is not ready for rehearsal. It is—”

“Yours?” Elias stepped closer. “Or is it?”

A beat of silence. The air thickened.

Then Licht laughed. It was a rich, genuine sound—at odds with the tension in his shoulders. “You truly believe you can conduct your way into my symphony? Impressive audacity.”

He strode to the podium. Picked up the baton Elias had left. Handed it back with both hands, as if presenting a sacred relic.

“Here,” he said, eyes gleaming. “Let’s hear what you’ve learned from me.”

Elias took the baton. The wood was cold.

He raised it.

The orchestra waited.

Then, without a cue, he brought it down—not with authority, but with precision, slicing through the silence like a blade.

The opening chords of *Requiem for a Lost Dawn* filled the hall.

Licht’s face went slack.

The tempo was wrong. Too slow. Too heavy. The harmonies dragged, weighted with grief Elias had never intended.

But the musicians played. They had no choice.

The performance was a ruin.

Licht’s breathing grew shallow. He stepped forward, mouth opening—then closed. His cane tapped the floor once, twice.

Elias didn’t stop. He conducted with his whole body now, arms slicing the air, hips pivoting to drag the rhythm into something darker, slower, truer.

Clara’s bow hovered over the strings. She didn’t play.

Isabella scribbled furiously, her lips moving as if tasting the disaster.

At the first measure’s end, Licht reached out and grabbed Elias’s wrist.

The baton trembled between them.

“Enough,” Licht said, voice low. “You are not fit to conduct your own funeral march, let alone my work.”

Elias met his gaze. Held it.

Then he let the baton slip from his fingers.

It clattered onto the podium.

Licht didn’t pick it up.

He let it lie.

For a long moment, no one moved. The music hung in the air, broken and unfinished.

Then Clara raised her violin.

She played the next phrase—correctly this time. Bold. Clean.

The orchestra followed.

Licht stepped back. His face was pale beneath the rouge.

Elias walked out of the Golden Hall without looking back.

Chapter 3

Elias’s attic smelled of old paper and lemon oil. The single window let in a slant of autumn light that turned the dust motes into floating gold. He laid the score across the table, smoothing each page with the flat of his hand, humming the corrected bassline, fingers tapping the rhythm against his thigh.

The knock came twice. Not urgent. Not soft. Expected.

Clara stood in the doorway, her coat damp at the shoulders, violin case in one hand. She didn’t step inside.

“I heard about today,” she said.

Elias didn’t look up. “Did you?”

“The Philharmonic is buzzing. Licht is furious. Isabella’s already writing her first draft.”

“And you?”

She shifted her weight. The heel of her boot scuffed the floorboard. “I played. Correctly. After you left.”

“A small mercy.”

Clara exhaled through her nose. “He called me into his office this morning. Shut the door. Sat me down like I was a student with a failing grade.”

Elias finally looked at her. Her coat was new—wool, dark green, the kind made for conductors. A gift? Or a shield.

“What did he say?”

“That if I defended you again, he’d strip my concertmaster title. Publicly. Permanently.” She lifted her chin. “I stood there. Didn’t cry. Didn’t flinch.”

“Good.”

She waited. He didn’t fill the silence.

“You could’ve stayed,” she said. “Fought. Called his bluff.”

“I did. It didn’t work.”

Clara’s fingers tightened around her violin case. “You could’ve *explained*.”

Elias turned back to the score. “Explain what? That I wrote *Requiem* when I was twelve? That Licht knew it then and stole it later? That the Philharmonic’s greatest symphony is actually a Voss composition?” He tapped the crest embossed on the cover—three interlocking treble clefs. “The world doesn’t care about truth. Only credit.”

Clara’s throat moved. “You’re giving up.”

“I’m waiting.”

“For what?”

He didn’t answer.

She set the violin case on the table. The wood creaked under the weight. “You’re not a fool, Elias. But you’re behaving like one.”

Elias picked up a pencil. Scratched a tempo marking in the margin—*largo, con dolore*.

Clara watched him for a long moment. Then she reached into her coat pocket. Pulled out an envelope. Slid it across the table.

“Licht’s autumn festival program,” she said. “He’s debuting his new symphony. *Symphony of the New Dawn*. Three months from now.”

Elias didn’t touch it.

“That’s not an invitation,” he said.

“No. But this is.” She pointed to the envelope. Inside, peeking from the seam, was a single concert ticket. Embossed with gold lettering: *Private Performance. By Invitation Only.*

Clara’s fingers lingered near the envelope. “He’ll never know you’re there.”

Elias picked up the ticket. Turned it over. The back was blank.

“Where?”

“His villa. In the Wienerwald. Next Saturday.”

Elias set the ticket down. Then he slid the envelope back across the table.

Clara’s eyebrows lifted. “Take it.”

“I don’t want your charity.”

“It’s not charity. It’s a warning.”

“Then warn me.”

She hesitated. Then, quietly: “Don’t go.”

Elias stacked the pages of *Requiem* into a neat pile. Tapped them on the table to align the edges.

“You’re afraid,” he said.

Clara didn’t deny it. “I’m afraid of what happens when you stop hiding.”

He set the score aside. Picked up the ticket. Tucked it into his waistcoat pocket.

Clara turned toward the door. Her coat flared as she moved, the green wool catching the last of the light.

“Clara.”

She paused.

“Thank you,” he said. “For the warning. For the honesty.”

She nodded once. Then she was gone, the stairs groaning under her weight.

Elias waited until the front door of the shop below clicked shut. Then he reached into his own coat pocket—felt the cold weight of the silver pocket watch, the one with the Voss crest engraved on the back.

He hadn’t given it to her.

He hadn’t put it in her coat.

But it was there now, resting against her gloves, a small, deliberate theft.

She would find it later.

Or she wouldn’t.

Elias set the watch on the table beside the envelope. Then he picked up the score again. And began to hum.

Chapter 4

The Golden Hall smelled of polished marble and old money. Gregor Licht stood at the conductor’s podium, baton raised, eyes fixed on the strings. The Vienna Philharmonic sat in hushed anticipation, bows poised, breaths drawn. A single rehearsal. Just one more run-through before the autumn festival.

Isabella Dvořáková perched in the third row, notebook open, pen hovering. The critics’ box was packed—wooden benches creaking under shifting weight. Every glance, every whispered word, every rustle of fabric felt like an audience for Licht.

He lowered the baton.

The opening bars of *Symphony of the New Dawn* surged forward, brass blazing, timpani cracking like thunder. The sound swallowed the hall whole. Light’s shoulders rolled with the music, his body a metronome of precision. The Philharmonic responded—woodwinds threading through the storm, cellos anchoring the storm, violins soaring like birds released from cages.

Isabella scribbled furiously. A line of sweat traced Licht’s temple, but he didn’t wipe it. He didn’t have to. Perfection required sacrifice. Even sweat.

At the break, the board members clustered around the stage like bees to a queen. Clothes rustled. Cufflinks glinted. One man, round-bellied and red-faced, pumped Licht’s hand so hard his cufflinks nearly flew off.

“Gregor, Gregor, *magnificent*. This will be the talk of Europe.”

Licht accepted the praise with a tight smile. “We’re only just beginning. The adagio must speak in whispers, not shouts.”

Isabella edged closer, notebook clutched to her chest. “Maestro, a word?”

Licht turned, fingers adjusting his cuffs. “Isabella. Always welcome.”

She didn’t bother with small talk. “The opening motif—it feels familiar. Almost *hauntingly* so.”

Licht’s smile didn’t waver. “Nonsense. It’s entirely original. I’ve been crafting it for months.”

“Of course.” She flipped a page. “But if one were to hear a faint echo of *Requiem for a Lost Dawn* in those opening notes… well, that would imply a debt to another’s genius, wouldn’t it?”

Licht’s fingers twitched. The baton, still in his hand, tapped his thigh once, twice.

“Elias Voss has a habit of seeing things that aren’t there,” Licht said. “A desperate man clings to any straw. But you—you have taste, Isabella. You wouldn’t mistake plagiarism for homage.”

She tilted her head. “I wouldn’t.”

The rehearsal resumed. Licht stepped back to the podium. The baton fell.

Three measures in, Clara Weiss’s violin cut through the chaos. A wrong note—shrill, out of place. The orchestra faltered. Woodwinds stumbled. Brass choked on the air.

Licht froze mid-gesture. His baton hovered, useless.

Clara lowered her violin. “My apologies, Maestro. A misfinger.”

Licht’s jaw tightened. “Take it from the top.”

They did. Again. And again. The same note tripped each time. Clara’s bow hand trembled. The first violin section shifted in their seats.

Isabella watched, pen frozen over her notebook. Then she closed it.

The rehearsal ended. The musicians packed up their instruments. Licht remained on the podium, staring at the empty stage as if willing the mistakes to vanish. The board members dispersed, murmuring their farewells.

Isabella lingered, pretending to adjust her glove.

“Gregor,” she said softly. “May I see your score?”

Licht didn’t turn. “Certainly not. Not until the festival.”

“I only meant—”

He cut her off. “You meant nothing. You only meant to plant the seed of doubt. Mission accomplished.”

Isabella didn’t flinch. “I only meant to serve the truth.”

Licht finally looked at her. His eyes were cold. “The truth is what I say it is.”

She nodded, retreated.

Licht waited until the last critic left. Then he descended the stage steps, baton tucked under his arm. The score lay spread across the conductor’s desk, pages secured with brass clips. He approached, scanning the sheets.

Halfway through the first movement, his fingers stilled.

A mistake. A blot of ink on the page. Not his.

He flipped the page. The ink was different. Darker. Thicker. Familiar.

His name—Gregor Licht—handwritten at the top, crossed out in red ink. Everywhere.

The pages were smudged with fingerprints. Some lines were annotated, phrases crossed out, new markings in the margins. Not Licht’s handwriting. Not the Philharmonic’s.

*Elias Voss* had been here.

Licht’s breath hitched. His fingers trembled as he thumbed through the score, searching for the worst offense. Page 47. A passage from *Requiem for a Lost Dawn*. Scribed in Licht’s own hand, but now annotated with corrections—notes Licht had never written.

His chest burned.

The baton slipped from his fingers. Clattered to the floor.

The hall was empty. No witnesses. No audience.

Only the score, his stolen symphony, and the ghost of a Voss.

Chapter 5

The rehearsal hall of the Musikverein reeked of rosin and damp wool. Late morning light slanted through the high windows, striped the orchestra with gold and ash. Gregor Licht stood at the podium, baton poised, his reflection caught in the polished wood of the music stands. Around him, the Vienna Philharmonic sat in silence, bows resting on knees, fingers tapping quiet rhythms on instrument cases.

Clara Weiss adjusted her music on the stand. A single sheet fluttered loose and she bent to retrieve it, her dark hair slipping forward to hide her face.

Licht’s baton fell.

The strings began, soft and deliberate. The oboe entered, high and clear. Licht’s left hand shaped the air, guiding dynamics only he could hear. The flutes hesitated. A wrong note—thin, exposed. The clarinetist frowned. Licht’s shoulders tensed.

He stopped.

“Again,” he said.

The oboe player exhaled through his nose. Footsteps shuffled. Someone coughed into a cupped hand.

Licht counted off. The music started anew. The same phrase stumbled. The horn section traded glances. Clara’s fingers tightened on her bow.

“No,” Licht snapped. “Not like *that*.”

A violinist near the front desk shifted in her seat. “Maestro, perhaps a slower tempo—”

“Silence.” Licht’s voice cut like glass. “We do not dictate the tempo. The music dictates. It is already written. It is perfect.”

The clarinetist cleared his throat. “With respect, the transition from the second to third movement feels—”

“Stick to your part.” Licht’s eyes didn’t leave the score. “Or find another orchestra.”

Silence settled like dust.

Clara lowered her violin. She placed the bow on the stand and stood. “I’ll take the passage.”

Licht turned. “You?”

“My section has the melody.” She adjusted the chinrest. “I can guide the phrasing.”

The musicians shifted. Some exchanged glances. Others kept their faces blank.

Licht’s grip on the baton tightened. “This passage has been rehearsed for weeks.”

“Then it should come easily.” Clara lifted her violin, arched the bow. “Begin.”

Licht hesitated. Then he lowered the baton.

The strings played the phrase. Clara stepped into the space between desks, her bow leading the melody, shaping each note with her wrist. The oboe followed. The clarinets breathed in unison. The music flowed.

Licht watched, his expression unreadable. His jaw worked slightly, as if chewing the moment.

Clara finished the passage and lowered her violin. “Thank you.”

The orchestra exhaled. A few musicians stifled sighs of relief.

Licht didn’t acknowledge her. He flipped a page in the score. “Continue.”

They played on. The brass swelled. The timpani rolled. Licht’s baton sliced through the air, sharp and commanding. But the ensemble tightened around Clara’s guidance. The balance improved. The sound grew richer.

Halfway through the third movement, a wrong note cracked through the hall. Dark. Sharp. Every eye turned to the percussionist, who stared at his marimba as if it had betrayed him.

Licht froze. Baton suspended.

The musicians held their breath.

Then Clara stepped forward. “I’ll take the passage.”

Licht’s fingers twitched. “No.”

“Maestro, the ensemble is—”

“It is *my* ensemble.” His voice was low, dangerous. “I conduct. I lead. I *decide*.”

Clara held his gaze. The air between them thickened.

A cellist cleared his throat. “Perhaps a short break?”

Licht didn’t look away from Clara. “Five minutes. Then we finish this.”

The musicians gathered their instruments. Cases snapped open. Chairs scraped. Footsteps echoed in the hall as the orchestra dispersed, whispering among themselves. Only Clara remained, standing beside her music stand, violin tucked under her arm.

Licht turned his back on her and stared at the score. His reflection in the music stands wavered with each shallow breath.

Clara stepped closer. “Gregor.”

He didn’t turn. “What?”

“You’re forcing it. The symphony isn’t ready.”

“It will be.” His voice was quiet. “For the festival.”

“It’s your festival.”

He finally looked at her. “Is it?”

A door banged at the far end of the hall. Musicians’ voices faded down the corridor. The clock on the wall ticked.

Clara lowered her voice. “You heard Isabella. The annotations in your score.”

Licht’s fingers curled into fists. “She’s a vulture.”

“She’s right about one thing. Elias’s fingerprints are all over it.”

“Then let him come forward.” Licht’s voice was a blade. “Let him claim it. Let the world hear his *gauntlet*.”

Clara didn’t flinch. “He won’t. He can’t.”

“Why not?” Licht’s eyes burned. “Because he’s a coward? A fraud? A ghost?”

“Because he’s dead.” Clara said it flat, final.

Licht stared at her. Then he laughed—a short, sharp sound. “That’s convenient.”

Clara held his gaze. “He disappeared three years ago. No one’s seen him since. No one hears from him. Not even his old contacts.”

Licht’s smile didn’t reach his eyes. “Then how did he annotate my score? How did he leave his mark in the margins?”

“I don’t know.” Clara’s voice was steady. “But the world believes he’s gone. And you’re using that belief to steal what isn’t yours.”

Licht stepped closer. “Careful, Clara. You’re close to insubordination.”

She didn’t back down. “I’m close to the truth.”

Licht’s mouth twisted. “Maybe we need Voss after all.”

He turned and strode toward the exit, baton tucked under his arm. The door slammed behind him.

Clara exhaled. She picked up her violin case and slung the strap over her shoulder. The hall felt colder without him.

She left the rehearsal space, descending the wide staircase to the lower corridor. The Musikverein’s marble floors gleamed under the electric lights. She passed the coat room, the green room, the door to the musicians’ lounge.

At the end of the corridor, a narrow door led to Licht’s private study. He kept it locked. Only the board and the cleaning staff had keys.

Clara froze.

The door stood ajar. A sliver of darkness beyond.

She approached. Pushed it open.

The study was wrecked. Books pulled from shelves. Chairs overturned. Papers strewn across the floor. The desk drawers hung open, empty.

Her pulse jumped.

Then she saw the baton.

Laying on the desk, snapped in half. A clean break, as if done with purpose.

Her breath caught.

A servant’s mistake? A burglar? Impossible. The Musikverein had no records of break-ins. The staff were vetted weekly.

She stepped closer. The snapped baton gleamed under the desk lamp.

A shadow moved in the hallway behind her.

Clara spun.

No one.

Only the empty corridor, the distant hum of the orchestra resuming in the hall above, the weight of the broken baton in her hand.

She bent to retrieve it.

A sound echoed down the corridor—footsteps. Fast. Light. Moving away.

Clara straightened, baton in hand, and followed.

Chapter 6

The underbelly of the Musikverein smelled of old paper and steam pipes. Clara descended the narrow servants’ staircase, her boots clicking against worn stone. She clutched the broken baton in her coat pocket, the wood warm from her grip. A folded program peeked from her sleeve—a souvenir from 1918, when Elias had debuted *Requiem for a Lost Dawn* under Licht’s name.

A radiator hissed in the corridor. She knelt, running her fingers along the pipes. The metal was cold except for one spot, just behind the grill. Her nails caught on something: a scrap of paper, wedged into the gap. She pulled it loose.

The program was torn, edges singed. In the margin, in Elias’s jagged script:

*You played it once. Play it again.*

Her stomach tightened. She folded it and tucked it into her pocket next to the baton.

A floorboard creaked above her.

Clara froze. The light above flickered. Shadows stretched long across the corridor. She exhaled through her nose and stood. No one followed. Just the hum of the furnace, the distant murmur of the orchestra rehearsing the next piece.

She left the corridor, stepping into the backstage hallway. The air smelled of hair pomade and rosin. Clara adjusted her coat and headed toward the dressing rooms, then paused. A door stood slightly ajar—the one to the storage closet where old programs and sheet music were kept. It didn’t usually open from the inside.

She pushed it.

Inside, a single shelf sagged under stacks of yellowed scores. A broom leaned against the wall, its bristles stiff with dust. No one here.

Then she saw it.

On the bottom shelf, half-hidden under a stack of *Nutcracker* parts, lay a score. Cream-colored paper, edges uncut, but the ink was fresh—still glistening. The title page read:

*Requiem for a Lost Dawn*
Elias Voss

Clara dropped to her knees. Her fingers trembled as she lifted the first page. The handwriting was unmistakable—Elias’s, precise, angular, the notes etched with a confidence she hadn’t seen in years.

She turned the pages. Every note, every orchestration mark, every dynamic was there. Complete. Unchanged.

But it wasn’t just the music that caught her breath. It was the crest pressed into the title page—a swan in flight, wings spread over a lyre.

The Voss family crest.

Clara’s pulse roared in her ears. She snapped the score shut. The spine cracked loudly in the silent room.

A shadow moved in the hallway outside.

She stuffed the score into her bag and stood, smoothing her coat. The door swung open.

A stagehand—young, unfamiliar—peeked in. “Fräulein Weiss? Maestro Licht requests your presence in Studio Three.”

Clara nodded. “Thank you. I’ll be right there.”

The stagehand hesitated, then left. His footsteps faded down the hall.

Clara exhaled. She pulled the program scrap from her pocket and tore it into smaller pieces, dropping them into the broom closet’s waste bin. Then she zipped her bag tight.

She didn’t hear the footsteps again until it was too late.

A hand clamped over her mouth. Another arm wrapped around her waist, yanking her backward. Clara dropped her bag. The score flew from it, pages scattering across the floor.

She kicked back, her heel connecting with a shin. A grunt. The grip loosened just enough.

She wrenched free and spun.

Isabella Dvořáková stood there, breath coming fast, her dark eyes burning. “You’ve been hiding things, Clara. And I want to know why.”

Clara’s heart hammered. She didn’t lower her guard. “You followed me.”

“You were in Licht’s study.” Isabella stepped forward. “I saw you. And now you’re carrying a score. *His* score, pasted with *your* fingerprints.”

Clara didn’t answer. She kept her eyes on Isabella’s hands, watching for a strike.

Isabella smirked. “Fine. Keep your secrets. But remember—I decide who gets invited into the Golden Hall. And who doesn’t.” She turned on her heel. “Enjoy the festival, Clara. It’ll be your last.”

The door slammed behind her.

Clara stood in the dim light, surrounded by scattered pages of *Requiem*. The ink was wet. The music was alive.

And someone had just tried to silence her.

Chapter 7

The brass section shuffled into Studio Three like cattle herded to slaughter. Their coats carried the tang of wet wool and brass polish, their boots scuffing the worn parquet. Gregor Licht stood at the podium, baton raised. The silence wasn’t respectful. It was wary.

“You’re late,” he said.

The first trombone player, a gaunt man with a permanent squint, cleared his throat. “Apologies, Maestro. Streetcar delays.”

Licht’s baton didn’t lower. “Every rehearsal this week. Same excuse.”

A young horn player shifted on her chair. “Not just the trams, sir. We all heard it.”

A murmur rippled through the section. The clarinets in the back exchanged glances.

Licht’s jaw tightened. “Heard what?”

The trombonist exhaled through his nose. “Last night. In our sleep. Like the music was played inside our heads.”

A double bass player in the corner muttered, “Ghost rehearsal.”

Licht’s baton snapped down. The score on the stand before him—his own symphony, the one soon to debut—rattled. “Nonsense. You’re imagining things.”

But the euphonium player wasn’t done. He stood, his chair scraping. “Then explain why every note we heard in our dreams was from *Requiem*. Every dynamic, every crescendo. And when we woke, we could hum the damn thing.”

Silence. Not even a bow creaked.

Licht’s fingers whitened around the baton. “You’re tired. Overworked. This is stress-induced hallucination.”

The tuba player, a mountain of a man with a red face, finally spoke. “We’re not dancers in a vaudeville show, Maestro. We know what we heard.”

Licht’s voice dropped, dangerously calm. “Then prove it. Play the opening motif.”

The brass section exchanged glances. The horn player lifted her instrument. Her fingers hovered over the valves.

Nothing.

No music. No phantom melody.

The tuba player swallowed. “It’s gone.”

Licht’s baton hovered. “Gone.”

“Like it was never there at all.”

The words hung in the stale air of the studio. Outside, a street organ played a waltz in the distance. Wrong season. Wrong mood.

Licht’s gaze swept over them, lingering on each face. The brass section sat frozen, their instruments cold in their laps. He turned to the door.

A stagehand stood there, face pale. “Maestro Licht?”

No one moved.

The stagehand swallowed. “They’re asking for you in the foyer.”

Licht didn’t respond. His eyes were fixed on something beyond the stagehand’s shoulder.

A whisper traveled through the room. Not spoken aloud, not quite. A murmur that started at the back and slithered forward.

*Elias.*

Then louder.

*Elias.*

Then a chorus.

*Elias. Elias. Elias.*

The brass section didn’t speak. They didn’t gasp. They simply stared at the door, at the empty foyer beyond, at the way the light slanted through the high windows like it was searching for something.

Or someone.

Licht’s baton trembled. Just once. Then steadied.

He stepped off the podium, coat tails snapping behind him. The brass players watched him go. They didn’t look at each other. They didn’t breathe.

The last thing they saw was Licht’s back, rigid, disappearing through the doorway.

The door didn’t close all the way.

A draft slipped through the gap, rustling the sheet music on the stands. The words on the top page—*Symphony in D Minor: A New Dawn*—seemed to shift in the flickering light.

Then the door swung shut on its own.

Chapter 8

The boardroom smelled of beeswax and old cigar smoke. The walls were lined with mahogany and gilt frames holding portraits of dead maestros. Clara Weiss stood between two empty chairs, her bow case clutched tight in one hand, her posture flawless. She had dressed for battle in a high-necked black dress, the one she wore when she played Bruch’s Violin Concerto No. 1 in G Minor—controlled, precise, unshakable. Today, it was armor.

Gregor Licht sat at the head of the table, fingers steepled. Three other members of the Philharmonic board flanked him, their faces unreadable. The fourth seat, usually occupied by Isabella Dvořáková, was empty.

“Concertmaster Weiss,” Licht said, “you have been brought before this committee under charges of gross professional misconduct.”

Clara did not sit. “I’m aware of the allegations.”

“Allegations?” Licht’s fingers twitched, then flattened against the table. “Your erratic behavior has disrupted rehearsals. You’ve undermined the authority of this institution. You’ve sided with a known plagiarist.”

“A plagiarist?” Clara’s voice stayed level, but the knuckles on her bow case whitened. “You mean the man who claims credit for a symphony he didn’t write?”

A board member—a violinist with a silver cravat—shifted in his seat. Licht didn’t blink.

“That man,” Licht said, “is not on trial today. You are. And your loyalty is suspect.”

Clara looked at the empty chair where Isabella should have been. “Where is she?”

“Critic Dvořáková is not a member of this committee,” Licht said. “But her latest review may interest you.”

He slid a newspaper across the table. Clara didn’t take it. Not yet.

“Read it aloud,” Licht ordered.

She unfolded the paper. The headline read: *Has Vienna’s Own Mozart Been Silenced by the Philharmonic’s Vanity?* The date was today. Her pulse didn’t jump. She read:

*“When Maestro Gregor Licht takes the stage this autumn to unveil his long-awaited symphony, he will be performing not musical innovation, but theft. For those who have followed the evolution—or devolution—of his *Requiem for a Lost Dawn*, the parallels to an obscure manuscript from 1848—the lost *Voss Requiem*—are not just uncanny. They are damning. If Licht did not compose this work himself, who did? And more importantly, why has he spent five years pretending otherwise?”*

She lowered the paper.

Licht’s smile was thin. “Curious, isn’t it? A critic once called you ‘the conscience of the Philharmonic.’ Now she accuses its leader of fraud.”

Clara refolded the paper carefully. “Isabella doesn’t accuse lightly.”

“Yet she printed words that could destroy this institution.”

“Or save it from a liar.”

One of the board members—a young woman in a dark green dress—spoke quietly. “Maestro Licht, should we not be concerned? If this is true—”

“It is not true,” Licht snapped. “It is a smear campaign by a disgruntled former lover and a recluse playing at grandeur.”

Clara met his gaze. “Then why are you stripping me of my rank?”

Licht leaned back in his chair. “Because your erratic conduct has made you unreliable. You let emotion dictate your bowing. You questioned the maestro in public. You visited Voss in his attic.”

“He’s a colleague.”

“He’s a ghost.” Licht stood, the chair scraping. “And you have been seen with him. Multiple times. The board cannot tolerate such recklessness.”

Clara set the bow case on the table. The clack of wood against mahogany echoed like a gavel.

“Then remove me,” she said. “Break my contract. Blacklist me. But know this: when the truth comes out—and it will—your name will be the one dragged through the mud.”

Licht didn’t flinch. “Truth is a luxury we cannot afford. Not when we stand on the brink of artistic ruin.”

The silver-cravat man cleared his throat. “A motion is on the floor. By a vote of four to zero—”

Clara didn’t wait for the rest. She picked up her bow case and turned toward the door.

“Concertmaster Weiss,” Licht said, voice hardening, “you are stripped of your title effective immediately. Your office will be reassigned. Your solo passages in the autumn program—”

Clara paused at the door. “Yes?”

“—will be played by concertmaster Hanzler.”

She didn’t look back. “As you wish.”

The door closed behind her with a quiet click.

---

Isabella Dvořáková sat in Café Central’s rear salon, the one with the red leather banquettes and the low light that made ink indistinguishable from blood. She didn’t touch her coffee. She never did. She dipped a biscuit into it, let it soften, then lifted it to her lips without breaking eye contact with the man across from her.

He was young, probably not yet thirty, with slicked-back hair and a suit that cost more than her annual salary. His name was Felix, and he worked in the Philharmonic’s archives.

“You’re certain?” Isabella asked.

Felix slid a manila folder across the table. “Page 57 of the 1848 Voss estate inventory lists a ‘symphonic score in D minor, untitled, attributed to Elias Voss.’ No opus number. No performance history. But the description matches *Requiem for a Lost Dawn* almost word for word.”

Isabella opened the folder. A black-and-white photograph showed a yellowed ledger page. The handwriting was elegant, precise—familiar.

She closed it. “And the original?”

“Missing. Rumor says it was burned in the ’48 uprising. But the inventory confirms it existed.”

Isabella tapped the folder. “Then Gregor Licht is not the genius he claims to be. He’s a thief.”

Felix shrugged. “Or a plagiarist. Or both.”

She picked up the folder. “Prepare a statement. Print it under my name. Say that the Philharmonic’s autumn festival must either credit the rightful composer or risk cultural fraud.”

Felix hesitated. “Maestro Licht will retaliate.”

“He already has,” Isabella said. “He just removed Clara Weiss. That’s an act of war.”

She stood, tucking the folder under her arm. As she walked past the mirrored wall, she caught her reflection—sharp cheekbones, dark eyes, hair pulled back like a blade. She looked like a woman who had just drawn blood.

Outside, rain began to fall, turning the streets slick and the gas lamps into golden halos. She hailed a cab without breaking stride.

The driver opened the door. Before she got in, she paused.

A boy on a bicycle rode past, ringing his bell. Behind him, a newspaper boy shouted: *Extra! Extra! Philharmonic scandal erupts!*

Isabella smiled. Not broadly. Just a flicker, like a match struck in the dark.

She got into the cab.

---

The mail cart arrived at the Musikverein at three forty-seven. The clerk sorted the envelopes by urgency, then by prestige. The thick one with the government seal went to Licht’s office first.

He opened it at his desk, slit the envelope with a silver letter opener shaped like a conductor’s baton.

His eyes scanned the letter once. Then again.

He didn’t slam his fist on the table. Didn’t curse. He simply set the letter down, flat, as if it were a score he needed to memorize.

The autumn festival program must include a Voss work—*Requiem for a Lost Dawn*—

—or risk cultural scandal.

The words blurred. He blinked and read them again.

*Voss.*

Elias Voss.

The ghost in the attic.

The man who had haunted every rehearsal, every private thought, every sleepless night.

Licht crumpled the letter in his fist, then smoothed it out, pressing the creases flat with his palm. The paper resisted. Like the truth.

He reached for the telephone.

“Get me the Ministry of Culture,” he said. “No. Not the Ministry. The Chancellor’s office. Immediately.”

He listened to the dial tone, tapping the letter against his teeth.

Outside his window, the Wienerwald loomed green and indifferent, as if it had never heard of light or dark, of theft or justice.

Licht hung up. The dial tone died.

He stood, walked to the window, and stared down at the cobblestone courtyard where musicians hurried through the rain, their coats dark and their collars turned up against the storm.

He had lost Clara.

He had lost the brass section to phantom melodies.

And now, the state was forcing his hand.

He turned away from the window.

The letter on his desk trembled in the draft from the open window.

He picked it up, folded it carefully, and placed it in the center of his blotter.

Then he picked up the telephone again.

“Make an appointment,” he said. “With Elias Voss.”

Chapter 9

Elias Voss polished the horsehair bow with the pad of his thumb, dragging it slowly across the fine linen draped over his desk. The attic apartment smelled of old wood and lemon oil, the scent of neglect he had long since stopped noticing. The violin lay in pieces on the desk—bridge removed, strings slack. He wasn’t fixing it. He was preparing it. For what, no one knew yet.

The knock came three times, deliberate, not rushed. Too heavy for a musician. Too practiced for a delivery boy.

Elias didn’t look up.

The door swung open without waiting. Gregor Licht stepped inside, closing it behind him with the care of a man who didn’t want to be seen entering. His coat was still damp from the rain outside, the fabric clinging to his shoulders. He didn’t remove it.

"You reek of arrogance," Elias said, finally looking up. He set the bow aside. "It’s a terrible scent. Shouldn’t be legal."

Licht didn’t smile. His jaw tightened.

"Your apartment smells like dust and failure," Licht said. "That’s legal, unfortunately."

Elias plucked a rosin cake from the desk, weighed it in his palm. "Then we’re both criminals. How delightful."

Licht crossed the room in three strides. He didn’t sit. He didn’t offer a hand. He simply stood over Elias, close enough that the older man could see the moisture on his temples, the dark circles beneath his eyes—signs of sleepless nights, not passion.

"I offered you a position once," Licht said. "Years ago. You refused."

"You offered me a seat in your orchestra as a second violin," Elias said. "I declined. I had better offers."

"Not from me."

"Correct. Because you have none to make."

Licht exhaled through his nose. It wasn’t a laugh.

"You could have been great," he said. "But you chose to be a ghost. A man who writes music and hides like a rat in these attics."

Elias picked up a screwdriver, turned it slowly in his fingers. "I chose survival. You chose theft."

The word hung between them. Licht didn’t flinch.

"You’re a fraud," Licht said. "And the world is beginning to notice."

Elias set the screwdriver down. "Then the world is finally seeing what you’ve worked so hard to obscure."

Licht’s fingers curled into a fist. "You have no proof."

"Don’t I?" Elias leaned back in his chair. The old wood creaked. "Isabella Dvořáková printed your plagiarism today. The brass section hears my music when they play your so-called genius. Clara Weiss was stripped of her rank for defending me. And now the Ministry of Culture demands you credit the rightful composer—or risk scandal."

Licht’s face didn’t change. But his fingers twitched.

"So what?" he said. "We weather the storm. We rewrite the program. We let history forget."

"You can’t rewrite history," Elias said. "You can only burn it."

Licht turned, walked to the small window that looked over the courtyard of the old music shop below. The glass was warped, distorting the view of the brick wall across the alley.

"You’re right," Licht said. "But I can offer you something else."

Elias didn’t respond.

Licht didn’t look back. "I can offer you credit. Full authorship. A place on the program. A commission for a new work. We’ll debut it together. Vienna will hear the truth—Gregor Licht and Elias Voss, the greatest composer duo of our age."

Elias picked up the violin scroll, turning it in the light. The wood was pale, almost ivory. It had been carved by his great-grandfather.

"You want to share a legacy," Elias said. "But you don’t want to surrender the throne."

Licht finally turned. His eyes were cold, but his voice wasn’t.

"No one surrenders," he said. "We negotiate."

Elias set the scroll down. "What do you want in exchange?"

"You," Licht said. "Silence."

A long silence filled the attic. The rain pattered against the window. Somewhere below, a clock chimed the half-hour.

Elias reached for the rosin again. He broke off a small piece, crumbled it between his fingers.

"Silence is a high price," he said. "Especially for a man who has spent his life making noise."

Licht stepped closer. "I’m not asking you to stop composing. I’m asking you to stop pretending it’s yours."

Elias looked up. His expression was unreadable.

"Is that what you believe?" he asked. "That it was ever mine to pretend?"

Licht didn’t answer.

Elias stood slowly, the chair scraping against the floorboards. He crossed to the small desk where his violin lay disassembled. He picked up the bridge, tested its weight in his palm.

"Some debts cannot be repaid in notes," he said.

Licht’s eyes flickered. Not with understanding. With something sharper.

"You’ve always been a poet," Licht said. "But poetry won’t feed you. It won’t restore your family’s name. It won’t stop the world from laughing when it finds out you’re nothing but a ghost in a dead empire’s shadow."

Elias placed the bridge back on the violin. He didn’t tighten the strings.

"You’re right," he said. "Poetry won’t do any of that."

Licht waited. When Elias didn’t continue, he stepped forward again, voice lowering.

"So take the offer. Take the credit. Take the life you should have had."

Elias picked up the polishing cloth, ran it over the violin’s belly. The wood gleamed.

"I had a life once," he said. "Before you stole it."

Licht’s face darkened. "This isn’t about me. This is about legacy. About what endures."

Elias folded the cloth carefully, set it down.

The attic felt smaller. The air thicker.

Licht’s voice dropped. "You could conduct the premiere. You could stand beside me. You could be remembered as the savior of this symphony, not its thief."

Elias looked at him. Really looked.

"You want me to be your alibi," he said.

Licht didn’t deny it.

The silence stretched. Then Elias walked to the desk, picked up the candle stub in its brass holder. He lit it with a match from his pocket. The flame bloomed, casting long shadows across the walls.

He set the candle down near the edge of the desk, close to the stack of handwritten scores.

Licht watched him, wary now.

"You walk out that door," Elias said, "and you take your rage with you. You take your threats. You take your stolen notes. But you leave the candle. Because some debts are paid in fire."

Licht didn’t move.

Elias stepped back. "Make your choice, Gregor."

Licht stared at the candle. At the flames licking the air. At the scores that could burn in seconds.

Then he laughed. It was a dry, humorless sound.

"You always were dramatic," he said.

He turned toward the door.

Elias didn’t see him leave.

He was watching the flame.

It stretched higher, hungry.

When the door clicked shut behind Licht, Elias still didn’t move.

The flame curled toward the edge of the desk. Toward the scores.

Toward the Voss crest stamped in gold leaf on the top sheet.

The fire touched paper.

A corner blackened.

Elias exhaled, slow and steady.

Then he picked up a glass of water from the desk.

And watched the flames lick closer.

Chapter 10

The cellar beneath *Haus der Klänge* was cold. Elias had unlocked it with a key no one else knew he had, a brass thing that had sat in a drawer for thirty years. The lock gave with a wet click, the sound swallowed by humidity and the weight of forgotten music.

He descended the narrow stairs, candle in hand, its flame steady against the draft. The cellar was a tomb of paper and silence. Shelves lined the walls, crammed with ledgers bound in cracked leather. Dust swirled in the air as he moved.

He set the candle on a barrel topped with a sheet of glass. The glass was cold. The barrel smelled of resin and old glue.

Elias lifted the first ledger. The spine cracked. *1823–1848: The Voss Compositions*.

His fingers traced the embossed crest on the cover: a lyre wreathed in laurel, the Voss motto barely visible beneath centuries of grime—*Per Musam Ad Astra*. Through music to the stars.

He opened it.

The first page was a dedication:

*To my daughter, on her fifth birthday, so that she may always hear her name in the wind.*

The handwriting was his great-grandmother’s. The ink had faded to sepia.

Elias turned the page.

Each ledger contained a full score, handwritten in ink so fresh it looked like it had been written yesterday. *Symphony No. 7 in D Minor*, *Sonata for Violin and Piano No. 12*, *Prelude to the Eclipse of the Divine*.

And there, bound in red ribbon, was the unmarked original of *Requiem for a Lost Dawn*, its pages numbered in Elias’s own script. The crest was stamped in gold leaf on the cover. The paper smelled like lavender and time.

He flipped to the last entry.

*July 1848: Requiem for the Fallen House. Composed in exile. To be played only when the Voss name is restored.*

Beneath it, a single line in newer ink:

*Hidden beneath Haus der Klänge. The key is in the drawer.*

Elias’s breath caught. He hadn’t written that line. Not in 1848. Not ever.

He checked the ledger’s endpapers. A key was tucked into a paper sleeve, its bow shaped like a lyre. The metal was tarnished, but the teeth were sharp.

A draft sighed through the cellar. The candle flame dipped.

Elias didn’t move.

He set the ledger down and lifted another. *The Lost Opus: A Century of Unpublished Voss Works*. The spine groaned as he opened it.

It was a catalog. Every composition the House of Voss had ever written, listed by date, dedication, and premiere status. Some were crossed out. Others were marked *Anathema*—never to be played.

A ledger slipped from his hands. It thudded onto the barrel, the glass sheet ringing like a struck tuning fork.

Elias picked it up. The glass was cracked.

He ignored it.

He pulled down another ledger. *1889–1901: The Silent Years*. This one was all piano études, dedicated to a woman named Clara—no last name.

He turned a page. A dried rose pressed between two scores fluttered to the floor.

He bent to pick it up. The stem was brittle. The petals were brown.

Footsteps echoed above.

Elias froze.

The footsteps stopped. A door creaked open. Light spilled down the stairs.

Clara stood silhouetted at the top, holding a candle. The flame lit her face from below, sharpening her cheekbones, darkening her eyes. She was wearing Licht’s coat—the wool was too big, the sleeves swallowing her hands.

She didn’t speak.

Elias didn’t stand.

The two candles cast long, wavering shadows on the cellar walls.

Clara descended one step. The wood groaned under her weight.

“You lied,” she said. Her voice was steady, but the candle trembled in her grip. “You said you had nothing left. You said it was all burned.”

Elias set the rose on the barrel. He closed the ledger. The spine snapped shut.

“No,” he said. “I said I had one thing left. This.”

He gestured to the shelves. To the ledgers. To the scores.

Clara took another step down. The hem of Licht’s coat brushed the stair.

“You’re the heir,” she said. It wasn’t a question.

Elias didn’t answer.

She took another step. The candlelight caught the crest on her lapel—the Philharmonic’s crest, not Voss’s. She wasn’t wearing it for him.

“Gregor said you were a fraud,” she said. “He said you were nothing. A ghost in a dead house.”

Elias picked up the ledger with the dried rose. He ran his thumb over the dedication.

“Gregor Licht lies like other men breathe,” he said. “It’s the only way he can stand himself.”

Clara’s fingers tightened around the candle holder. The wax dripped onto the stair.

“Then you’re not—”

“I’m everything he claimed I wasn’t,” Elias said. “And more.”

She descended another step. The coat was Licht’s. The crest was Licht’s. But her posture was wrong. She wasn’t his soldier.

She was his executioner.

Elias set the ledger down. He folded his arms.

“Why are you here, Clara?”

She didn’t answer at first. She descended the last step. The cellar swallowed her.

“I came for the truth,” she said.

Elias smiled. It didn’t reach his eyes.

“The truth is a terrible thing,” he said. “It burns. It scars. It leaves nothing behind.”

Clara held up the candle. The flame danced between them.

“Then let it burn,” she said.

Elias stepped back. His heel struck a ledger. It toppled, pages splaying open on the floor.

Clara’s eyes flicked to the scores. To the crest. To the lyre, the laurel, the name that had been erased from history.

She lifted the candle higher.

“Gregor was right about one thing,” she said. “You’re not who you claimed to be.”

Elias didn’t deny it.

The cellar was silent except for the drip of wax and the distant hum of the music shop above.

Clara took a step forward.

The ledger under Elias’s boot crunched.

He didn’t move.

She set the candle on the barrel beside him. The flame grew taller between them, casting their shadows up the walls like twin giants.

“Then who are you, Elias Voss?”

He met her gaze.

The ledger under his boot was the one with the dried rose.

He pressed down.

The paper split. The spine cracked. Pages peeled apart like dead skin.

Clara’s eyes widened.

Elias’s voice was quiet.

“I’m the man who’s going to bring the house down.”

Chapter 11

Isabella Dvořáková’s office smelled of printer’s ink and coffee gone cold. The walls were lined with music journals, their spines creased from frequent handling. A single unlit cigarette rested on an ashtray beside a half-empty glass of pálinka. The typewriter clattered in the corner, its keys striking like hammers of judgment.

Isabella stood by the window, watching the Vienna Philharmonic’s brass section march into the Musikverein like soldiers returning from a lost campaign. She adjusted her glasses and exhaled through her nose.

Gregor Licht stood in the doorway, his overcoat still damp from the morning rain. His face was a mask of forced civility, but his knuckles whitened around the handle of a briefcase.

“We need to talk,” Isabella said.

Licht stepped inside. The door clicked shut behind him.

“You’ve already written it,” he said.

She turned, her fingers resting on a stack of folded papers. “I’ve written the truth.”

Licht set the briefcase down. It thudded against the wooden floor.

“You don’t understand the damage this will do.”

Isabella picked up the pages, tapped them against her palm. “I do. And so will they.”

“They’ll see it as a stunt. A woman’s jealousy.”

She laughed, a sharp brittle sound. “Or they’ll see it as proof that the Philharmonic has been lying to them for years.”

Licht’s eye twitched. “You have no proof.”

“Wrong.” She tossed the pages onto the desk. “I have the ledger. From Haus der Klänge. The one with the Voss crest.”

Silence.

The clock on the wall ticked like a metronome counting down to ruin.

Licht’s fingers flexed. “That ledger doesn’t exist.”

“It does.” Isabella opened her desk drawer and pulled out a single sheet of paper. “And Elias Voss’s signature is on it. Right next to his great-grandmother’s.”

Licht didn’t move. But his breath hitched.

Isabella slid the paper across the desk. “Page 173. *Requiem for a Lost Dawn*. Composed by Elias Voss, conducted by Gregor Licht. Sounds familiar?”

Licht didn’t touch it. “You’re fabricating.”

“Am I?” She leaned forward, her voice dropping to a whisper. “I have the original score, Gregor. The one with the family crest. The ink’s still wet. And it’s dated 1915—three years before you ever put pen to paper.”

Licht’s jaw tightened. “You have no right—”

“Right?” Isabella cut him off. “I have the right to report the truth. And Vienna deserves to know why their beloved maestro is conducting music he didn’t write.”

She reached for the typewriter. Her fingers hovered over the keys.

“Stop this,” Licht said, his voice low and dangerous. “Or I will ruin you.”

Isabella didn’t look up. “You already have. By stealing from a ghost.”

The typewriter’s carriage snapped back into place with a sharp *crack*.

Licht’s hand shot out and slammed the carriage down. The machine jolted, a key sticking as the ribbon tore.

Isabella didn’t flinch.

Licht stepped back, his chest rising and falling like a bellows. “You think this is over? The board meets tomorrow. They’ll see you as a vengeful woman, a critic with a grudge.”

“They’ll see the truth.”

“They’ll see a liar.”

Isabella smiled. “Then let them.”

Licht turned and left, the door slamming so hard a framed photograph of Beethoven rattled on its hook.

Isabella exhaled slowly. She lit her cigarette, the flare of the match illuminating the exhaustion in her face. She knew the battle wasn’t won. It had only just begun.

She turned back to the typewriter and began to type.

---

The Philharmonic boardroom was a cavern of mahogany and velvet, the air thick with the scent of beeswax and old cigars. The members sat in a semicircle, their faces grim as tombstones. At the center of the table, the chairman tapped a gavel against the wood. The sound echoed like a death knell.

Isabella stood before them, her notebook open, her pen poised. She wore a simple black dress, no adornment—deliberate, austere. She wanted them to see the messenger, not the message.

“You’ve read the proofs,” she said. “You know what I’m about to publish.”

A murmur rippled through the room. One man, a violinist with a silver cravat, shifted in his seat. Another, a cellist, stared at his hands.

The chairman cleared his throat. “Fraulein Dvořáková, we appreciate your passion, but this is a matter for the Philharmonic alone.”

“It’s a matter for Vienna.”

Gregor Licht stood from his place along the wall, his posture rigid, his jaw set like granite. “This is a fabrication. Elias Voss is a myth, a ghost story. The Philharmonic will not be dragged into his shadow.”

A woman in the front row, a flutist, spoke up. “But the ledger—”

“Is stolen,” Licht said. “Planted by a desperate man.”

Isabella set the ledger on the table. It landed with a thud. The gold crest on the cover gleamed under the chandelier.

The room fell silent.

Licht didn’t look at it. “That ledger is a forgery. And if it isn’t, then it belongs to the Philharmonic as part of Elias Voss’s estate. Which it doesn’t. Because he has no heirs.”

Isabella opened the ledger to a marked page. “Here. Page 173. Requiem for a Lost Dawn. Composed by Elias Voss. Conducted by Gregor Licht. Performed 1918.”

She slid it toward the chairman.

The chairman picked it up, his fingers trembling slightly. He read. Then he read aloud, his voice rough.

“‘*Requiem for a Lost Dawn*. By Elias Voss.”

He looked up. “Gregor.”

Licht’s face was pale. “It’s not—”

“Then explain the dates,” Isabella said. “Voss died in 1915. You premiered this in 1918.”

The chairman set the ledger down. “Gregor. Is this true?”

Licht’s hands were clenched at his sides. “It’s more complicated than that.”

“It’s plagiarism,” the flutist said.

The room erupted. Voices overlapped. The chairman banged the gavel again. “Silence!”

Isabella stood firm. “The Philharmonic stands for purity. For truth. For art that rises above the man. But when the man steals the art—what are you left with?”

A man in the back, a horn player, stood up. “We need a vote.”

The chairman looked at Licht, then at Isabella, then back at the ledger.

He nodded.

Isabella closed her notebook. The click of the clasp was the only sound in the room.

---

The Golden Hall was dark, the stage lights off, the vast space swallowed by shadow. Only a single lamp flickered near the conductor’s podium, casting a pool of light on the worn wood. The air smelled of rosin and old wood, of sweat and time.

Gregor Licht stood in the center of the stage, his back to the empty seats. He had torn off his coat, his sleeves rolled to the elbows. His hands shook as he pulled a cigar from his waistcoat pocket. He lit it with a match, the flame trembling.

The match dropped to the floor.

He didn’t bend to pick it up.

A door creaked open behind him. Light spilled onto the stage. A board member stepped inside, his face drawn.

“Gregor.”

Licht didn’t turn. “They voted.”

The board member cleared his throat. “They stripped you of artistic direction.”

Licht exhaled smoke through his nose. “Of course they did.”

“Isabella’s article goes to print at dawn.”

Licht crushed the cigar under his heel. “Then let it burn.”

The board member hesitated. “Gregor, Elias Voss—”

“Is a ghost,” Licht said. “A dead composer haunting us from the past.”

The board member opened his mouth, then closed it. He stepped back into the shadows and the door clicked shut.

Licht was alone.

He walked to the podium, placed both hands on the wood. The surface was cold beneath his palms. He closed his eyes.

He had lost. The Philharmonic was gone. His reputation was in tatters.

But he still had one thing.

He descended the steps to the backstage area, moving through the dim corridor lined with busts of dead composers. Beethoven. Mozart. Schubert. Their marble faces watched him pass.

He reached the bust of Wagner. It was heavy, cold. He gripped it by the neck and lifted.

A compartment in the pedestal yawned open.

Inside, wrapped in oilcloth, was a score. Thick. Aged. The cover was stamped with a crest—lyre, laurel, a motto fading into gold.

Gregor Licht exhaled.

He knew that crest.

He pulled the score free. The oilcloth peeled away.

*Requiem for a Lost Dawn.*

The ink was fresh. The paper smelled of lavender—Elias Voss’s scent.

Licht flipped through the pages. The markings were Elias’s. Unmistakable.

His hands shook.

This wasn’t just proof. This was a weapon.

He could use it. He could destroy Elias Voss with it. Expose him as a fraud, a thief of his own work.

Gregor Licht smiled.

He would.

He tucked the score under his arm and began to walk back toward the Golden Hall.

Somewhere in the shadows, he heard a whisper.

But he didn’t stop. He never stopped.

The stage lights flickered on behind him.

Chapter 12

The Golden Hall was empty. Not a single soul in the thousand seats, not a rustle of silk or a cough from the upper balconies. Just the vast hollow space, the curved ceiling swallowing sound, the polished floor cold underfoot. The only light came from a single brass lamp on the conductor’s podium, its glow pooling on the worn wood like spilled brandy.

Elias Voss stood there, clutching a leather portfolio. The air smelled of rosin and lemon oil, of old paper and candle wax. He had rehearsed this moment a hundred times in his attic, standing before his mirror, bathed in lamplight. But now, with the scale of the hall pressing in, the weight of the score in his hands felt different. Real.

Gregor Licht emerged from the shadows near the orchestra pit, his overcoat draped over one arm. His face was a study in calculated calm—lowered brows, thinned lips, the faintest twitch at the corner of his mouth. He walked like a man who had already won, like a king entering his own domain.

“You’re early,” Licht said. “Or perhaps late. Depends on how you measure time these days.”

Elias didn’t answer. He unclasped the portfolio and let the lid fall back. The *Requiem* score lay exposed, its pages creased from years in hiding. The Voss crest—lyre, laurel, the motto *Per Aspera ad Astra*—was stamped in gold leaf on the cover.

Licht’s eyes flicked to it. Just once. Then he set his coat over the back of a folding chair and rolled up his sleeves. The movement was deliberate, meant to signal control.

“You always did like the dramatic,” Licht said, stepping onto the stage. His shoes made no sound on the wood. “Upstaging your own memorial concert. Irony isn’t lost on me.”

Elias held the score higher. “This was never yours.”

Licht stopped three paces away. Close enough to see the ink, close enough to smell the lavender in the paper. Close enough to know he was cornered.

“It was composed in 1915,” Elias said. “Three years before you ever claimed it. By me.”

“By a ghost,” Licht replied. “A man who died before the ink could dry.”

Elias smiled. “Then explain the ledger.”

Licht’s fingers curled into a fist. “A forgery. Coincidence. Or worse—a plant by someone who wanted my ruin.”

“Isabella Dvořáková disagrees.”

“Isabella has a vendetta.” Licht’s voice was smooth, practiced. “She sees plagiarism where there is none. She wants a scandal because she believes the Philharmonic should be led by talent, not tradition.” He stepped closer. “And she’s wrong.”

Elias didn’t flinch. “She published the truth. The board believes her.”

“Temporary.” Licht’s jaw tightened. “The Philharmonic will rebound. It always does. But *Requiem*? That’s finished. Over. Buried.”

“Not by you.” Elias held out the score. “It’s mine to reclaim.”

Licht laughed. A low, humorless sound. “You think a piece of paper changes anything? You think shouting *mine* in an empty hall makes it true?” He reached for the score.

Elias pulled it back.

Licht’s hand hovered. Then dropped. “You’re not even a conductor, Elias. You hide in your attic, scribbling notes no one will ever hear. You let me take the credit, let me take the standing, let me take the life that should have been yours.” His voice dropped. “And now you want it back?”

Elias didn’t answer. He didn’t need to. The score was answer enough.

Licht’s eyes darkened. “You’re a fool. You think this ends with a piece of paper? Fine. Take it. Burn it. It won’t bring your father back. It won’t restore your name.”

Elias’s breath hitched. Only once. But Licht saw it.

“Ah,” Licht said softly. “So you *do* remember.”

Elias tightened his grip on the score. The edges bit into his fingers.

Licht took another step forward. “I should have let the mob tear your family apart in ’48. Maybe then you’d understand what real loss looks like.”

Elias lunged.

The score tore in the struggle, a jagged line splitting the title page. Licht grabbed Elias’s wrist, twisting. The portfolio clattered to the floor, pages spilling like dead leaves.

“You think I haven’t dreamed of this?” Licht hissed, close enough for Elias to taste the cigar smoke on his breath. “Every night, waking up to applause that wasn’t mine. Every concert where they stood for *me*—not you. And now you come with your *proof*?” He spat the word. “It’s too late. The Philharmonic board has already decided. They’ve voted. I’m done.”

Elias wrenched free. He staggered back, clutching what was left of the score. The pages fluttered in his hands, edges singed from old candles, ink bleeding in places.

Licht straightened his cuffs. “You lose.”

Elias stared at him. Then, slowly, he lowered the score.

For a heartbeat, Licht relaxed. A smirk tugged at his lips.

Then Elias smiled.

The paper in his hands caught fire.

A single match wouldn’t do it. A wick wouldn’t either. This was something else. The flames rose blue at the edges, then curled upward like wings—thin, translucent, glowing. The score burned without heat, without ash, the flames shaping themselves into something alive.

Licht stumbled back. “What—?”

A figure emerged from the fire.

A score.

A second score.

Hidden beneath the first, its pages untouched by time, its ink dark and fresh. The title read *Sonata for a Silent Dawn*, dated 1919. The handwriting was unmistakable—flourishes, dynamics scrawled in Elias’s own hand.

Licht’s breath came in ragged gasps. “That’s—”

“Yours?” Elias said. “No. Mine.”

The fire consumed the first score completely, the *Requiem* reduced to embers that lifted like smoke and vanished into the rafters. The music swelled—not from instruments, not from speakers, but from the air itself. A single note, sustained, vibrating the glass in the windows, trembling the wood beneath their feet.

Gregor Licht screamed.

And Elias Voss did not look away.

Chapter 13

Clara Weiss’s apartment smelled of lavender and old newsprint. The walls were lined with shelves of violin scores, their spines cracked from use. A single lamp cast a yellow circle on the writing desk, where Isabella Dvořáková sat with her back to the door. She didn’t turn when Clara entered. The weight of the shattered pocket watch in Clara’s palm was the only sound.

“You came,” Isabella said. Her voice was flat, stripped of the authority she wielded in her reviews.

Clara didn’t answer. She set the watch on the desk between them. The glass face was split in half, gears scattered across the wood like fragments of a broken promise.

Isabella stared at it. “You found it.”

“He left it in his coat at the Philharmonic,” Clara said. “Careless. Or arrogant.”

Isabella picked up one of the gears, rolling it between her fingers. “He trusted me. With everything.”

“And you took it to Licht.”

Isabella’s hand stilled. “I only wanted to expose the truth. That he wasn’t the genius he pretended to be. That the Philharmonic was built on lies.”

Clara leaned forward. “So did he. And you helped destroy him.”

Isabella’s breath hitched. Just once. “I didn’t know he’d—”

“He what?” Clara cut in. “Burn the scores? Rewrite history? He always knew the truth would bury him. He just didn’t expect you to hand it to him.”

Isabella’s fingers tightened around the gear. “I thought I was saving the Philharmonic from a fraud.”

Clara picked up the other half of the watch. The hands were frozen at 3:47. The exact time Licht had taken the podium in 1918 to claim *Requiem* as his own. “Fraud implies choice. He didn’t have one.”

Isabella’s jaw clenched. “Then what is he?”

Clara turned the watch over. Engraved on the back: *For Gregor, who never needed the truth. L.* “A man who made his own chains.”

Isabella stood abruptly, knocking over her teacup. The liquid spread across the desk, dark and relentless, soaking into the pages of an open score below. “You don’t understand. The board was going to vote him out anyway. I just gave them the push.”

Clara wiped the spill with her sleeve. “And now?”

“Now he’s ruined.” Isabella’s voice cracked. “And he knows it was me.”

Clara looked up. “Do you regret it?”

Isabella didn’t answer. She walked to the window, pressed her palm against the cold glass. Outside, the rooftops of Vienna stretched toward the twilight, their chimneys like skeletal fingers.

Clara picked up the last intact sheet of paper from the desk. A review draft. Isabella’s byline stood at the top: *A Symphony of Lies: The Truth Behind the Maestro.*

She folded it in half, then in half again. “You should have let it burn.”

Isabella’s shoulders trembled. “I only wanted to be right.”

Clara dropped the folded paper onto the desk. “You wanted to be the one who mattered.”

Isabella didn’t deny it.

A knock at the door.

Both women froze.

Not a knock. A hammering. Three sharp strikes, then silence, then two more. The sound of a man who had run out of patience.

Isabella turned slowly. “Who is it?”

Clara already knew. She crossed the room, her slippers whispering against the floorboards. She opened the door.

Gregor Licht stood in the hallway, his coat unbuttoned, his hair wild. A violin case hung from one hand, its leather scuffed from use. His face was gaunt, his eyes red-rimmed. The man who had once filled a concert hall with his presence now looked like a ghost.

“Clara,” he said. His voice was raw. “I need to play.”

Chapter 14

The boardroom of the Vienna Philharmonic smelled of polished mahogany and the faint metallic tang of old coins. Elias stood at the head of the table, his black coat still damp from the autumn drizzle that had soaked the cobbles outside. The members of the board sat in rigid silence, their faces etched with the kind of patience that comes from decades of deferred gratification. At the far end, Clara Weiss stood with her violin case at her feet, her fingers tapping a restless rhythm against her thigh.

Elias did not sit.

"I will not ask for forgiveness," he said. "I will not grovel. I will not pretend this was anything but theft."

A board member—Herr Bauer, the treasurer—adjusted his spectacles. "Maestro Licht has offered us a path forward. A collaboration. Shared credit."

Clara’s fingers stilled.

Elias smiled. It was not a kind expression. "Shared credit. How generous." He reached into his coat and placed a single sheet of paper on the table. The board leaned forward as one.

The title at the top read: *The House of Voss: A Legacy of Composition.*

Beneuer exhaled through his nose. "This is historical revisionism."

"No," Elias said. "It’s a ledger." He tapped the page. "Every composition credited to Gregor Licht since 1911—when he took over the Philharmonic—was composed by my family. My father. My uncle. And, yes, me."

A woman at the end of the table—Frau Vogel, the secretary—touched her throat. "You’re claiming the *Requiem for a Lost Dawn*?"

"Not claiming," Elias said. "Returning." He slid another sheet across the table. A list of titles, dates, and scores hidden in his apartment. Clara’s breath hitched when she saw *Sonata for a Silent Dawn* near the top.

Clara stepped forward. "Elias, please."

He did not look at her.

Bauer cleared his throat. "And your condition?"

Elias folded his hands. "Restore Clara Weiss as concertmaster. Add her signature to the program for next month’s festival. And in the program notes, publish the Voss name alongside every stolen work."

Frau Vogel flipped through the ledger. "This would dismantle the Philharmonic’s modern reputation."

"Your reputation was built on lies," Elias said. "I’m offering you a choice: reclaim your integrity, or cling to the fraud."

Silence settled over the room. Then Bauer nodded slowly. "We accept your terms."

Elias bowed his head. Just once.

Clara’s shoulders sagged.

The board members began to murmur among themselves, gathering their papers, preparing to leave. Elias reached for the ledger, but Bauer held up a hand. "One moment, Maestro."

He opened a drawer and produced a small velvet box. Inside lay a silver pocket watch, its chain coiled neatly beside it.

Elias’s fingers twitched.

"This was found in Licht’s possession," Bauer said. "He claims it was a gift from a benefactor. But the engraving suggests otherwise."

Clara stepped closer. The watch face was cracked, the hands frozen at 3:47.

Elias did not touch it. "You have your watch."

"And you have your name," Bauer said.

The board filed out, their footsteps muffled by the thick carpet. Only Clara remained. She picked up the ledger and tucked it under her arm. "You could have asked me."

Elias walked to the window, staring out at the Musikverein across the square. Its golden façade gleamed in the weak afternoon light. "Would you have believed me?"

Clara did not answer.

A door creaked open behind them. Light footsteps. Then a voice, smooth and practiced.

"Well. This is unexpected."

Gregor Licht stepped into the room. His coat was damp at the shoulders, his gloves still on. His eyes flicked from Elias to Clara and back. The ledger in Clara’s arms seemed to draw his gaze like a magnet.

Elias did not turn. "Gregor."

Licht’s smile was a blade unsheathed. "I hear you’ve been busy, Voss."

Clara shifted the ledger behind her back. "Leave."

Licht ignored her. "A ledger of compositions. How quaint." He took a step closer. "You think this changes anything?"

Elias finally turned. "It changes everything."

Licht’s gaze dropped to the empty space on the table where the watch had been. His fingers curled into fists. "You think you’ve won."

Clara moved between them. "Gregor—"

But Licht was already striding past her, his booted foot catching the edge of a chair. It scraped against the floor with a screech. Then he was at the piano in the corner of the room, his hands already hovering over the keys.

Elias watched, cold and still.

Licht’s fingers settled on the keys. He began to play.

Not his own work.

Not the *Symphony of the New Dawn* he had promised the board.

The opening bars of *Requiem for a Lost Dawn.*

Elias felt the weight of the future settle on his shoulders.

Chapter 15

The Golden Hall of the Musikverein exhaled. A collective breath released from four hundred throats, rippling through the air like the last shiver of a dying wind. Elias lowered his baton, his arms heavy with the weight of a hundred repetitions compressed into one perfect arc. The final chord of *Resurrection of a Lost Dawn* hung in the gilded space, suspended between the crystal chandeliers and the frescoed ceiling. Then it dissolved.

The audience erupted.

A storm of applause crashed over the stage, hands clapping together like shattering glass. Elias turned slowly, his baton still gripped in his right hand, his left raised slightly as if to temper the noise. His black tailcoat clung to his shoulders, damp with the exertion of conducting not just the music, but the silence that had come before it. The Voss crest glowed behind him, embroidered in silver thread on a crimson backdrop, its intricate lines now familiar to every eye in the hall.

Clara stood in the first violin section, her bow raised in salute, her face streaked with tears she would not wipe away. Beside her, Isabella Dvořáková sat ramrod straight, her gloved hands beating a precise rhythm against her program. Only Gregor Licht remained still. He sat in the third row from the back, his fingers curled around the armrests of his chair, his face a mask of polished fury.

Elias smiled.

He did not smile at the applause. He did not smile at the critics in the front rows, scribbling furiously. He did not smile at Clara’s quivering bow or Isabella’s sharp nod of approval.

He smiled at the watch.

The silver pocket watch lay on the conductor’s stand, its chain coiled neatly beside it. The hands were frozen at 3:47. The same moment Licht had stormed into his attic years ago, seized the original score of *Requiem for a Lost Dawn*, and declared it his own.
