Elias plucked it from the stand and closed it in his palm. The metal was cold, but it radiated warmth through his skin—proof that time could be reclaimed, that loss could be reversed.

A hand touched his elbow. Clara. “They’re calling for you,” she said, her voice raw. “The encore.”

Elias did not look at her. “Not now.”

“Elias.”

He turned. Her eyes were wide, her lips parted as if she might say more. Instead, she nodded toward the wings. The orchestra had already begun to shuffle their music, the percussionist tapping a restless rhythm with his mallets.

Licht stood in the aisle.

He did not clap. He did not stand. He simply watched, his gaze locked onto Elias, his jaw tight beneath his silver-streaked beard. In his left hand, he held a single sheet of paper.

Elias pocketed the watch and stepped down from the podium. The applause followed him like a shadow, thickening the air with its insistence. Clara fell into step beside him, her violin case bumping against his coat.

“You’re doing it again,” she said. “Walking into the trap.”

Elias did not slow. “There is no trap.”

“Then why is he here?”

They reached the wings. The orchestra’s murmur died as Elias appeared. The audience hushed, expectant. A single spotlight cut through the haze of incense and sweat, settling on his face.

Elias raised his baton. The orchestra tensed, poised for the encore.

Licht stepped into the light.

He held the paper high, as if presenting evidence in a courtroom. The sheet fluttered in his grasp, the edges crisp despite his grip.

“A moment,” Licht called, his voice cutting through the hall like a blade. “Before we proceed to the next masterpiece.”

Elias lowered his baton. The orchestra exhaled in unison, their bows hovering like birds mid-flight.

Isabella Dvořáková stood in the aisle, her notebook open, her pencil poised. The critics at the front turned in their seats, pens frozen over notebooks.

Licht cleared his throat. “I wonder if anyone has noticed the date.”

Clara stiffened.

Elias did not react. He stood perfectly still, his gloved hands clasped behind his back, his face a blank canvas.

Licht smiled. It was not triumphant. It was pitying. “This evening’s ‘magnum opus’—*Resurrection of a Lost Dawn*—was premiered exactly one hundred years ago today. By my grandfather. At this very hall.”

A murmur rippled through the audience. A few heads turned toward Isabella, who was already scribbling.

Licht pressed on. “And yet, we are asked to believe that a ghost has returned to claim credit for works that have enriched this institution for generations.” He let the paper drift to the floor, where it landed with a whisper. “I suppose it’s easier to rewrite history than to live with it.”

The spotlight flickered. The orchestra shifted, their bows lowering in unison.

Elias did not move.

Clara’s breath came fast. “Elias—”

He held up a hand. The gesture silenced her.

Licht stepped closer, his polished shoes clicking against the wooden floor. “You had your moment, Voss. But history is not a ledger to be balanced with sentiment. It’s a fortress. And you—” he tilted his head, studying Elias like a specimen “—are not its heir.”

The audience held its breath.

Elias reached into his coat. The watch in his palm felt heavier now, its edges biting into his skin.

He opened the watch.

The hands began to move.

Not clockwise. Counterclockwise. Backward, through the years, through the betrayals, through the silence.

The chime echoed through the hall—a single, deep toll of a funeral bell.

Then silence.

Licht’s eyes flickered to the watch. His face paled.

Elias snapped the watch shut. “Then let history remember the truth,” he said, his voice carrying across the hall like a conductor’s final command. “The Voss compositions were never lost. They were stolen. And tonight, they have been returned.”

The orchestra erupted into applause, but it was not for Elias. It was for Licht, who stood frozen in the spotlight, his mouth opening and closing like a landed fish.

Clara grabbed Elias’s arm. “We have to go. Now.”

Elias did not resist. He let her pull him into the wings, the orchestra’s applause following them like a receding tide.

Behind them, the Golden Hall buzzed with confusion, with murmurs, with the first stirrings of scandal.

Ahead, the stage door stood open, the Vienna night waiting beyond.

Clara pulled him into the corridor. Her fingers dug into his wrist. “Elias, for God’s sake—”

He stopped. Turned. Looked back at the Golden Hall through the doorway, its gilded doors ajar, its chandeliers still glowing.

Licht was gone.

The paper lay on the floor where he had dropped it, trampled now by retreating feet.

Elias smiled. Not at Clara. Not at the chaos. At the watch in his hand, its hands now moving forward again, ticking steadily toward the future.

Clara followed his gaze. “What did you do?”

Elias closed his fist around the watch. “I gave him back his time,” he said. “And then I took mine.”
